items.title: Exploring a new dataset before modeling
items.scenario: A data scientist receives a CSV of customer features (age, tenure, monthly charges, churn label). Before training a classifier, they load it into pandas and run `sns.pairplot(df, hue="churn", diag_kind="hist")` to see which numeric features separate classes visually.
items.explanation: Pair plots surface linear and nonlinear separability across all feature pairs in one figure—faster than plotting each scatter individually.

items.title: Correlation heatmap for feature redundancy
items.scenario: An ML engineer notices two input features both measure usage intensity. They compute `corr = df[numeric_cols].corr()` and plot `sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm", center=0)` to spot |r| > 0.9 pairs before dropping redundant columns.
items.explanation: Heatmaps make symmetric correlation matrices scannable; high off-diagonal values warn about multicollinearity in linear models.

items.title: Comparing model scores across algorithms
items.scenario: After cross-validation, a team has AUC values for three models on five folds. They melt results to long form and use `sns.boxplot(data=results, x="model", y="auc")` to compare median performance and spread before picking a production candidate.
items.explanation: Categorical plots summarize distribution of metrics across runs—useful when evaluating models whose ROC-AUC varies by fold.

items.title: Checking residual distribution after regression
items.scenario: A analyst fits a linear model on housing data and plots `sns.histplot(residuals, kde=True)` to verify residuals are roughly symmetric and centered at zero— a quick sanity check before trusting coefficient p-values.
items.explanation: Distribution plots are the standard first look at whether model errors violate normality or homoscedasticity assumptions.
